verses.text: Abraham is old, well stricken in age, and the LORD has blessed him in all things. He calls his eldest servant — the steward of everything he owns — and makes him swear: my son must not marry a Canaanite. Go back to my kindred, and find him a wife there.

Abraham is old, well stricken in age, and the LORD has blessed him in all things. He calls his eldest servant — the steward of everything he owns — and makes him swear: my son must not marry a Canaanite. Go back to my kindred, and find him a wife there.

Abraham is old, well stricken in age, and the LORD has blessed him in all things. He calls his eldest servant — the steward of everything he owns — and makes him swear: my son must not marry a Canaanite. Go back to my kindred, and find him a wife there.

Abraham is old, well stricken in age, and the LORD has blessed him in all things. He calls his eldest servant — the steward of everything he owns — and makes him swear: my son must not marry a Canaanite. Go back to my kindred, and find him a wife there.

The servant asks: what if she won't come back with me? Should I take Isaac there? No, says Abraham — never bring him back. The LORD will send His angel before you. If she won't follow, you are released from the oath. Don't drag Isaac out of the land of promise.

The servant asks: what if she won't come back with me? Should I take Isaac there? No, says Abraham — never bring him back. The LORD will send His angel before you. If she won't follow, you are released from the oath. Don't drag Isaac out of the land of promise.

The servant asks: what if she won't come back with me? Should I take Isaac there? No, says Abraham — never bring him back. The LORD will send His angel before you. If she won't follow, you are released from the oath. Don't drag Isaac out of the land of promise.

The servant asks: what if she won't come back with me? Should I take Isaac there? No, says Abraham — never bring him back. The LORD will send His angel before you. If she won't follow, you are released from the oath. Don't drag Isaac out of the land of promise.

The servant asks: what if she won't come back with me? Should I take Isaac there? No, says Abraham — never bring him back. The LORD will send His angel before you. If she won't follow, you are released from the oath. Don't drag Isaac out of the land of promise.

The servant sets out with ten camels and a sample of all his master's goods. He travels to Mesopotamia and arrives at evening, at the well outside the city — the very hour when the women come to draw water.

The servant sets out with ten camels and a sample of all his master's goods. He travels to Mesopotamia and arrives at evening, at the well outside the city — the very hour when the women come to draw water.

He prays: O LORD God of my master Abraham, send me good speed today. Let the young woman who offers to draw water not only for me but for all my camels — let her be the one. He stakes the future of the covenant on a girl's hospitality.

He prays: O LORD God of my master Abraham, send me good speed today. Let the young woman who offers to draw water not only for me but for all my camels — let her be the one. He stakes the future of the covenant on a girl's hospitality.

He prays: O LORD God of my master Abraham, send me good speed today. Let the young woman who offers to draw water not only for me but for all my camels — let her be the one. He stakes the future of the covenant on a girl's hospitality.
Before he finishes praying, Rebekah comes out — beautiful, a virgin, the daughter of Bethuel, Abraham's nephew's son. The answer was already on her way before the question was finished.
Before he finishes praying, Rebekah comes out — beautiful, a virgin, the daughter of Bethuel, Abraham's nephew's son. The answer was already on her way before the question was finished.

He runs to meet her: let me drink a little water. Drink, my lord, she says, and quickly lowers her pitcher. When he is done, she offers — I will draw for your camels also, until they have done drinking. Ten thirsty camels. She doesn't know what she has just agreed to.

He runs to meet her: let me drink a little water. Drink, my lord, she says, and quickly lowers her pitcher. When he is done, she offers — I will draw for your camels also, until they have done drinking. Ten thirsty camels. She doesn't know what she has just agreed to.

He runs to meet her: let me drink a little water. Drink, my lord, she says, and quickly lowers her pitcher. When he is done, she offers — I will draw for your camels also, until they have done drinking. Ten thirsty camels. She doesn't know what she has just agreed to.

He runs to meet her: let me drink a little water. Drink, my lord, she says, and quickly lowers her pitcher. When he is done, she offers — I will draw for your camels also, until they have done drinking. Ten thirsty camels. She doesn't know what she has just agreed to.
The man watches her in silence — wondering, weighing, holding his breath. He doesn't speak. He simply watches the answer to prayer unfold one bucket at a time.
When the camels are finished, he gives her a gold nose ring and two heavy gold bracelets. Then he asks: whose daughter are you? Is there room in your father's house for us to lodge?
When the camels are finished, he gives her a gold nose ring and two heavy gold bracelets. Then he asks: whose daughter are you? Is there room in your father's house for us to lodge?

I am the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, son of Nahor, she says — Abraham's grand-niece. Yes, we have straw and feed and room for the night. Then the servant bows and worships the LORD: blessed be the God of my master, who has led me straight to my master's family.

I am the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, son of Nahor, she says — Abraham's grand-niece. Yes, we have straw and feed and room for the night. Then the servant bows and worships the LORD: blessed be the God of my master, who has led me straight to my master's family.

I am the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, son of Nahor, she says — Abraham's grand-niece. Yes, we have straw and feed and room for the night. Then the servant bows and worships the LORD: blessed be the God of my master, who has led me straight to my master's family.

I am the daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, son of Nahor, she says — Abraham's grand-niece. Yes, we have straw and feed and room for the night. Then the servant bows and worships the LORD: blessed be the God of my master, who has led me straight to my master's family.

Rebekah runs home and tells her mother's house everything. Her brother Laban sees the gold on her wrists, hears her story, and runs out to the well himself. Why are you standing outside? Come in, blessed of the LORD.

Rebekah runs home and tells her mother's house everything. Her brother Laban sees the gold on her wrists, hears her story, and runs out to the well himself. Why are you standing outside? Come in, blessed of the LORD.

Rebekah runs home and tells her mother's house everything. Her brother Laban sees the gold on her wrists, hears her story, and runs out to the well himself. Why are you standing outside? Come in, blessed of the LORD.

Rebekah runs home and tells her mother's house everything. Her brother Laban sees the gold on her wrists, hears her story, and runs out to the well himself. Why are you standing outside? Come in, blessed of the LORD.

The camels are unloaded, the men's feet washed, food is set before them. But the servant refuses to eat until he has spoken. I will not eat until I have told my errand. The work of the LORD comes before bread.

The camels are unloaded, the men's feet washed, food is set before them. But the servant refuses to eat until he has spoken. I will not eat until I have told my errand. The work of the LORD comes before bread.

I am Abraham's servant, he begins. The LORD has blessed my master greatly — flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, donkeys. And Sarah bore my master a son in her old age, and to him my master has given everything.

I am Abraham's servant, he begins. The LORD has blessed my master greatly — flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, donkeys. And Sarah bore my master a son in her old age, and to him my master has given everything.

I am Abraham's servant, he begins. The LORD has blessed my master greatly — flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, donkeys. And Sarah bore my master a son in her old age, and to him my master has given everything.
And my master made me swear: do not take a Canaanite wife for my son. Go to my father's house, to my kindred, and bring a wife for him there. If she will not come, you are clear from this oath.
And my master made me swear: do not take a Canaanite wife for my son. Go to my father's house, to my kindred, and bring a wife for him there. If she will not come, you are clear from this oath.
And my master made me swear: do not take a Canaanite wife for my son. Go to my father's house, to my kindred, and bring a wife for him there. If she will not come, you are clear from this oath.
And my master made me swear: do not take a Canaanite wife for my son. Go to my father's house, to my kindred, and bring a wife for him there. If she will not come, you are clear from this oath.
And my master made me swear: do not take a Canaanite wife for my son. Go to my father's house, to my kindred, and bring a wife for him there. If she will not come, you are clear from this oath.

So I came today to the well and prayed: if you are prospering my journey, let the woman who offers to draw water for me and my camels be the one. Before I finished praying in my heart, Rebekah came with her pitcher and did exactly that.

So I came today to the well and prayed: if you are prospering my journey, let the woman who offers to draw water for me and my camels be the one. Before I finished praying in my heart, Rebekah came with her pitcher and did exactly that.

So I came today to the well and prayed: if you are prospering my journey, let the woman who offers to draw water for me and my camels be the one. Before I finished praying in my heart, Rebekah came with her pitcher and did exactly that.

I asked whose daughter she was, gave her the gold, and worshipped the LORD who had led me straight to my master's family. Now I am asking you: will you deal kindly and truly with my master? Tell me yes or no, that I may know which way to turn.

I asked whose daughter she was, gave her the gold, and worshipped the LORD who had led me straight to my master's family. Now I am asking you: will you deal kindly and truly with my master? Tell me yes or no, that I may know which way to turn.

I asked whose daughter she was, gave her the gold, and worshipped the LORD who had led me straight to my master's family. Now I am asking you: will you deal kindly and truly with my master? Tell me yes or no, that I may know which way to turn.

I asked whose daughter she was, gave her the gold, and worshipped the LORD who had led me straight to my master's family. Now I am asking you: will you deal kindly and truly with my master? Tell me yes or no, that I may know which way to turn.

I asked whose daughter she was, gave her the gold, and worshipped the LORD who had led me straight to my master's family. Now I am asking you: will you deal kindly and truly with my master? Tell me yes or no, that I may know which way to turn.

Laban and Bethuel answer together: this is from the LORD. We cannot say good or bad about it. Behold, Rebekah is before you. Take her, and go, and let her be your master's son's wife, as the LORD has spoken.

Laban and Bethuel answer together: this is from the LORD. We cannot say good or bad about it. Behold, Rebekah is before you. Take her, and go, and let her be your master's son's wife, as the LORD has spoken.

The servant bows again to the ground in worship. Then he brings out silver jewelry, gold jewelry, and clothing for Rebekah, and precious things for her brother and mother. Only after this is given does he and his men eat and drink and lodge for the night.

The servant bows again to the ground in worship. Then he brings out silver jewelry, gold jewelry, and clothing for Rebekah, and precious things for her brother and mother. Only after this is given does he and his men eat and drink and lodge for the night.

The servant bows again to the ground in worship. Then he brings out silver jewelry, gold jewelry, and clothing for Rebekah, and precious things for her brother and mother. Only after this is given does he and his men eat and drink and lodge for the night.

In the morning he says: send me back to my master. Her brother and mother say: let her stay with us at least ten days. He refuses: do not delay me — the LORD has prospered my way. They say: let us call the young woman and ask her.

In the morning he says: send me back to my master. Her brother and mother say: let her stay with us at least ten days. He refuses: do not delay me — the LORD has prospered my way. They say: let us call the young woman and ask her.

In the morning he says: send me back to my master. Her brother and mother say: let her stay with us at least ten days. He refuses: do not delay me — the LORD has prospered my way. They say: let us call the young woman and ask her.
Wilt thou go with this man? they ask her. Her answer is brief and brave: I will go. Two words decide the future of the covenant — and she speaks them on the same day she meets the messenger.

They send Rebekah away with her nurse, with this blessing: be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those that hate them. The same promise once given to Abraham now follows the woman walking into it.

They send Rebekah away with her nurse, with this blessing: be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those that hate them. The same promise once given to Abraham now follows the woman walking into it.

Rebekah and her maids mount the camels and follow the man. The servant has done his work; she is now journeying toward a husband she has never met. The road from Mesopotamia to Canaan is the second great journey of Abraham's family.

Rebekah and her maids mount the camels and follow the man. The servant has done his work; she is now journeying toward a husband she has never met. The road from Mesopotamia to Canaan is the second great journey of Abraham's family.

Isaac is in the field at evening, meditating — the Hebrew word can mean wandering thoughts, prayer, or quiet reflection. He looks up and sees the camels coming. At the same moment, Rebekah looks up and sees the man in the field.

Isaac is in the field at evening, meditating — the Hebrew word can mean wandering thoughts, prayer, or quiet reflection. He looks up and sees the camels coming. At the same moment, Rebekah looks up and sees the man in the field.
Who is this man walking in the field to meet us? she asks. It is my master, the servant says. So she takes her veil and covers her face. The first meeting carries her own quiet dignity.

The servant tells Isaac everything that has happened. And Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent. He takes her as his wife, and he loves her — and Isaac is comforted after his mother's death.

The servant tells Isaac everything that has happened. And Isaac brings Rebekah into his mother Sarah's tent. He takes her as his wife, and he loves her — and Isaac is comforted after his mother's death.
